Wheat Futures Jump as Speculators Pile In -- Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais


-- Wheat for May delivery rose 3% to $5.69 1/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Thursday, as traders were seen favoring wheat as a speculative commodity investment.

-- Soybeans for May delivery rose 0.7% to $11.56 1/2 a bushel.

-- Corn for May delivery fell 0.1% to $4.36 1/2 a bushel.


HIGHLIGHTS


Speculation Surge: Speculators jumped into wheat Thursday, said AgResource in a note. "An exact fundamental for the wheat rally is lacking, but the market has been technically based for most of the past week," said the firm.

AgResource dismisses weather issues in areas of the U.S. Midwest and in Western Europe as the principal cause of the rush.

"It's just too early to kill a wheat crop that is a grass and often has nine lives," the firm said.

As Expected: The USDA released its first outlook for how many acres of corn, soybeans and wheat farmers are expected to plant this spring. The outlook fell in line with what analysts anticipated, with corn acreage seen at 94 million acres and soybean acreage at 85 million acres. For corn that is a 4.8 million-acre decrease from last year, while for soybeans it is a 3.8 million-acre increase.

The USDA also projects another year of high yields for both. Corn yields are seen at 183 bushels an acre and soybean yields at 53 bushels an acre.

In Mother Nature's Hands: After the USDA released its agricultural outlooks, analysts now turn to watching the weather. If weather is generally favorable for farmers planting this spring and growing this summer, then U.S. farmers are likely to produce near-record levels of corn and soybeans similar to last year.

But analysts say that stable weather conditions aren't at all certain.

"Things could happen pretty quickly," said John Newton of the American Farm Bureau Federation. "We were in a pretty similar situation in 2019."

Excessive rainfall during spring 2019 derailed what was expected to be a sizable corn and soybean crop.


INSIGHT


Off Balance: Trump administration officials will attempt to address areas where they feel that trade remains unbalanced ahead of a planned joint review of the USMCA scheduled for July.

"There are areas of the USMCA that we want to take a look at that will focus more on agricultural trade deficits with Canada and agricultural deficits with Mexico," said Julie Callahan, the chief agricultural negotiator for the U.S. Trade Representative. Addressing attendees of the USDA's Agricultural Forum in Arlington, Va., Callahan said that the trade deficit and Canada's dairy tariff-rate quotas will be subjects that the U.S. will want to address.

Light Movement: The USDA's first projections for row crop prices in the upcoming growing season show a slightly higher average of prices per bushel for corn, soybeans and wheat.

The government said it expects the average corn price to rise 10 cents per bushel to $4.20/bushel in the 2026-27 marketing year. A 10-cent uptick to $10.30/bushel is forecast for soybeans, and average wheat prices are also expected to rise 10 cents, to $5 a bushel.

In a presentation at the USDA's Agricultural Outlook Forum, USDA chief economist Justin Benavidez said costs to produce crops are also expected to remain stubbornly high.

Showing Resilience: Issues with the U.S. farm economy may be smaller than many think, said Nate Kauffman, senior vice president with the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City.

In a presentation at the USDA Agricultural Outlook Forum, Kauffman said that agricultural land values have been resilient at historically high levels.

"If there was more fundamental weakness in agricultural markets, I think you'd see evidence of pressure on land values and cash rents," Kauffman told attendees. That doesn't mean areas of the farm economy aren't under strain, he cautioned, but many farmers have the assets needed to stay afloat in weak times like last week.


AHEAD


-- The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly export sales report at 8:30 a.m. EST Friday.

-- The USDA will host the second day of its 102nd annual Agricultural Outlook Forum in Crystal City, Va., with the floor opening at 8 a.m. EST.

-- The USDA is due to release its monthly Cattle on Feed Report at 3 p.m. EST Friday.


Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-19-26 1552ET

Sector Update: Health Care
New York drops plan to allow robotaxis outside the city in setback for Waymo
data.description:
New York State's governor has dropped her initiative after concluding there was not enough backing, particularly in the state legislature. The plan, unveiled in her State of the State address, was meant to allow commercial autonomous services outside New York City, under strict safety requirements and with local support. Waymo, Alphabet's self-driving unit, said it was "disappointed" by the reversal. Trials currently underway in Manhattan and central Brooklyn, authorized through March, are not affected.

The initiative drew strong opposition from unions, ride-hailing drivers and public-transport workers, who voiced concerns over safety and jobs. The Taxi Workers Alliance, which represents more than 28,000 drivers, welcomed a "sensible decision" and denounced the company's strategy. Waymo secured its first testing permit in New York in August and is currently operating a limited number of vehicles with safety drivers. The company says it provides about 400,000 rides a week in the United States and plans to launch a driverless service in several US cities and London.

Amid intensifying competition, Waymo faces Tesla, Zoox and Chinese players such as Apollo Go and WeRide, which are accelerating their international expansion. While some rivals have yet to roll out large-scale commercial service, pressure is mounting in the autonomous-vehicle market. The company has also reported several recent incidents, including a collision with a child near a school in Santa Monica that is under federal investigation. Another vehicle, operating in manual mode, was seen speeding along a residential street in Los Angeles.

AMD set to guarantee $300m loan to back Crusoe in AI
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) is preparing to guarantee a $300m loan to cloud start-up Crusoe to finance the purchase and deployment of its artificial intelligence chips, according to The Information. The deal, structured with Goldman Sachs, underscores intensifying competition among semiconductor makers to support the buildout of AI-focused infrastructure.

According to sources close to the matter, AMD would commit to re-leasing its own chips from Crusoe if the young company fails to attract enough customers, particularly amongst AI developers. The loan would be backed by the chips and related equipment, and AMD's guarantee would have helped secure an interest rate of about 6%, lower than what was expected without that support. Crusoe is currently installing AMD chips in a data center being built in Ohio by Canadian developer 5C, backed by Brookfield.

Founded in 2018 in the cryptocurrency sector, Crusoe has repositioned itself around AI infrastructure and is among the "neo-clouds" specializing in services tailored to companies in the industry. Its chief executive, Chase Lochmiller, had said he planned to buy $400 million in new AMD chips. Nvidia, AMD's main rival, is also ramping up deals and investments with start-ups, prompting questions on Wall Street about circular financial structures and the risk of an AI-driven boom.

J&J Explores $20 Billion-Plus Sale of Orthopedics Unit, Bloomberg Reports, Citing Sources
data.description:
--Johnson & Johnson is preparing a potential sale of its DePuy Synthes orthopedics unit, which it has been planning to separate, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

--The unit could be valued at more than $20 billion in a sale, the report said, citing the people.

--J&J is assembling documents and financials for DePuy Synthes before it meets with possible buyers in the coming weeks, and several large private equity firms are considering teaming up to buy out the unit, the people said, according to the report.

--A representative for J&J declined to comment, Bloomberg said.


Full article at https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-02-19/j-j-explores-20-billion-plus-sale-of-orthopedics-unit


Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Canadian dollar steadies near 13-day low as trade deficit narrows
data.description:
TORONTO, Feb 19 (Reuters) - The Canadian dollar steadied after six straight days of declines against its U.S. counterpart on Thursday as oil prices rose and data showed that Canada's trade deficit narrowed in December.

The loonie was trading nearly unchanged at 1.37 per U.S. dollar, or 72.99 U.S. cents, after earlier touching its weakest level since February 6 at 1.3714.

Canada's trade deficit narrowed to C$1.31 billion ($957 million) in December from a revised C$2.59 billion in November, as a jump in exports of unwrought gold helped lift total exports by 2.6%.

For 2025, exports edged down 0.2%, weighed by reduced exports to the United States after it began a trade war.

"Canada has thus far escaped the worst of the trade blow," Karl Schamotta, chief market strategist at Corpay, said in a note. "But officials on both sides of the border are preparing for acrimonious negotiations in the months ahead - and we think (currency) hedgers should be doing the same."

The United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, which has shielded much of Canada's exports from U.S. tariffs, is set for review by a July 1 deadline.

The price of oil, one of Canada's major exports, settled 1.9% higher at $66.43 a barrel as traders worried about escalating tensions between the United States and Iran, which have stepped up military activity in the oil-producing Middle East.

The safe-haven U.S. dollar added to recent gains against a basket of major currencies after U.S. data indicated the economy was stable, giving the Federal Reserve leeway to hold interest rates in check.

Canadian bond yields were little changed across the curve. The 10-year was down less than half a basis point at 3.226%, after touching on Tuesday a near seven-week low at 3.204%.

(Reporting by Fergal Smith;Editing by Alison Williams)

By Fergal Smith

Dollar advances after data implies stable labor market
data.description:
NEW YORK, Feb 19 (Reuters) - The dollar strengthened for a fourth straight session on Thursday after data indicated the economy was on stable footing, giving the Federal Reserve leeway to hold interest rates in check in the near-term.

The Labor Department said weekly initial jobless claims fell by 23,000 to an adjusted 206,000, below the 225,000 estimate of economists polled by Reuters. 

"It doesn't look like an economy suffering from higher rates. Even with the pressure from the White House to lower rates, that pressure doesn't really hit until May, so there's no real trend to this information right now," said Joseph Trevisani, senior analyst at FXStreet in New York.

"That's why we're headed back to a range. Without a reason to move, traders are going to be conservative."

TRADE DEFICIT WIDENED 

Other data from the Commerce Department showed the U.S. trade deficit widened to $70.3 billion, well above the $55.5 billion estimate. 

The dollar index, which measures the dollar against a basket of currencies, rose 0.19% to 97.88, with the euro down 0.14% at $1.1766. The four-day run of gains for the greenback would mark its longest streak since early January. 

The euro fell for a second straight day, extending declines after its biggest daily percentage decline since January 30 on Wednesday after the Financial Times reported that European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde was expected to leave her post before the end of her eight-year term. 

Four sources told Reuters that Lagarde has informed colleagues she remains focused on her job and she would tell them first if she was about to step down, a message they took to mean she was not about to resign.

Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said the labor market has remained "pretty resilient" and that the central bank is close on both mandates of maximum employment and stable prices. 

Markets are not pricing in more than a 50% chance for a rate cut of at least 25 basis points from the Fed until its June meeting, according to CME's FedWatch Tool.  

Fed minutes released on Wednesday showed policymakers were divided over where to take U.S. rates and suggested that the next chairman, due to start in May, would struggle to push through rate cuts.

Investors were mindful of a reported buildup of U.S. forces in the Middle East and a potential U.S.-Iran conflict, a concern that lifted oil prices. U.S. President Donald Trump said Thursday that "really bad things will happen" if no deal is reached with Iran and the United States will get a deal one way or the other.

Against the Japanese yen, the dollar strengthened 0.06% to 154.92 while sterling weakened 0.25% to $1.3457.

Bank of England interest rate-setter Catherine Mann said British inflation data published this week represented "good numbers" although there was not as much improvement in the underlying figures as the central bank had hoped to see.

(Additional reporting by Tom Westbrook in Singapore, Dhara Ranasinghe and Amanda Cooper in London; Editing by Anil D'Silva, Barbara Lewis and Deepa Babington)

By Chuck Mikolajczak
